articles.title: British Council offers masters scholarships in the UK exclusively for women
articles.summary: The British Council has opened applications for full master’s scholarships exclusively for women in science, technology, engineering, and mathematics (STEM). These scholarships are offered through the Women in STEM program and provide places at the University of Warwick and Aston University in England.
articles.insight:
This initiative signals growing institutional emphasis on gender equity in STEM recruitment, potentially influencing international students’ perceptions of inclusivity and opportunity—though its direct impact on overall demand remains uncertain without data on uptake or spillover effects across other destinations.  
Admissions teams should monitor whether similar targeted scholarships emerge in their regions and assess if prospective women in STEM applicants increasingly prioritize equity-focused support, while reviewing current outreach to ensure alignment with evolving diversity expectations.

articles.title: Hong Kong to establish global version of university entrance exam on pilot basis
articles.summary: Hong Kong’s education authorities plan to pilot an internationally benchmarked version of the city’s Diploma of Secondary Education (DSE) exam to enable foreign students to apply for admission to higher education institutions in Hong Kong and mainland China. Technical explorations and studies with relevant bodies on developing this international DSE version are currently underway, according to Secretary for Education Christine Choi Yuk-lin.
articles.insight:
Hong Kong’s pilot international DSE may gradually diversify global student pathways to Chinese-speaking higher education, potentially affecting demand for Anglophone degrees—though its scale, recognition, and uptake by foreign students remain uncertain and likely years from material impact.  
Admissions teams should monitor the pilot’s rollout timeline, participating institutions, and accreditation outcomes, particularly how mainland Chinese universities adopt it, as early signals could inform regional recruitment positioning and articulation pathway reviews.

articles.title: US: Student visa issuances fell by -36% in summer 2025; OPT uncertainty among factors affecting international student demand
articles.summary: US student visa issuances fell by 36% in summer 2025, with uncertainty surrounding the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme cited as a key factor affecting international student demand. The US government has renewed its focus on the OPT programme, and former Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, who was replaced on 5 March, had previously responded favourably to Senator Eric…
articles.insight:
The 36% drop in US student visa issuances during summer 2025—linked partly to OPT uncertainty—suggests growing caution among international applicants, though it remains unclear whether this reflects temporary policy anxiety or a deeper, sustained shift in destination preferences across key source markets.  
Admissions teams should track real-time application and enrolment trends by region and programme level, while closely monitoring US regulatory developments around OPT, visa processing timelines, and comparative messaging from peer destinations to assess evolving competitive positioning.

articles.title: New Zealand expands post-study work opportunities for international students
articles.summary: New Zealand plans to introduce a new Short Term Graduate Work Visa and extend eligibility for the Post Study Work Visa, with implementation scheduled for late 2026. No specific launch date has been confirmed, and the government’s international education strategy emphasizes managed growth over the coming years.
articles.insight:
New Zealand’s planned 2026 visa reforms—though still undefined in detail—may modestly improve its competitiveness for students prioritising post-study work, particularly from Asia and the Pacific, though demand shifts will depend on implementation clarity and comparative policy changes elsewhere.  
Admissions teams should track official guidance on eligibility criteria and duration of the new Short Term Graduate Work Visa, while benchmarking against evolving post-study work offers in the UK, Australia, and Canada to inform targeted messaging and agent training.

articles.title: UK denies visas to some winners of Chevening, its own most prestigious scholarship
articles.summary: The UK government has denied student visas to dozens of Chevening Scholarship winners, its most prestigious international scholarship program. This occurred after the UK imposed a blanket ban on student visas from four countries affected by war.
articles.insight:
This incident signals potential reputational and trust risks for the UK’s international education brand, possibly dampening demand from conflict-affected regions and amplifying scrutiny of visa policy consistency—though broader enrolment impacts remain uncertain pending data on affected cohorts and alternative destination uptake.  
Admissions teams should monitor shifts in application volumes from high-risk source countries, track competitor messaging around visa reliability, and assess whether institutional support services need strengthening for students navigating geopolitical visa uncertainty.
